zh-tw.title: 地獄笑話大全
zh-tw.description: 後勁很強的黑色幽默——命運的諷刺、人生的反差、那些讓你先笑再沉默的荒謬真相

en.title: Dark Humor Jokes
en.description: Jokes with a twist that hits differently — the kind that make you laugh first, then quietly stare at the ceiling

items.sourceNote: 算命財運諷刺，原創

zh-tw.content:
算命師看著阿順的掌紋，緩緩說：
「你這輩子，財庫豐厚。」

阿順大喜：「真的嗎？」

「對，豐厚——但是別人的財庫，不是你的。
你天生就是來幫別人增值的。」

阿順沉默了很久。
最後說：「那我能改命嗎？」

算命師：「能，但要先付我算命費——
順便也是在幫別人的財庫再充一次值。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每個月薪水月底就見底的朋友，他會先笑，然後沉默非常久——因為算命師說的每一句都是真話，只是包裝得很婉轉
variations: 進階版：「那哪輩子才輪到我的財庫？」算命師翻了翻書：「這個需要另外加算，費用另計。」

en.content:
The fortune teller studied A-Shun's palm and said slowly:
"Your treasury in this life will be plentiful."

A-Shun lit up: "Really?"

"Yes, plentiful — just someone else's treasury, not yours.
You were born to help other people grow their wealth."

A-Shun stayed quiet for a long time.
Finally: "Can I change my fate?"

Fortune teller: "You can. But first you'll need to pay my reading fee —
which will, again, be a contribution to someone else's treasury."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose paycheck disappears by the end of every month — they'll laugh, then go quiet for a while. Because everything the fortune teller said is true. Just kindly worded.
variations: Follow-up: "So which lifetime is mine?" Fortune teller flips through a book: "That requires a separate reading. Additional fee applies."

items.sourceNote: 生日蠟燭存在主義，原創

zh-tw.content:
你有沒有想過，生日蠟燭的一生其實很地獄？

它花了整整一年時間等待，
終於在最重要的那一天，被點亮——
成為全場最耀眼的存在。

然後大家唱完歌，
把它全部吹熄。

那是它的高光時刻，也是終點。

不過換個角度——
至少它確實發過光，
而且讓人許了願。
沒有它，願望去哪許？
zh-tw.editorNote: 生日的時候說，對方在吹蠟燭前一定會愣一秒——但最後那句「讓人許了願」可以讓這個笑話從地獄回到溫暖，端看你想留在哪個層次
variations: 蠟燭可能是最積極的存在主義者：清楚知道自己在燃燒，知道終將熄滅，還是全力燃燒到最後一刻——不是沒有選擇，只是選擇了燃燒。

en.content:
Have you ever thought about the tragedy of birthday candles?

They spend an entire year waiting,
then on the most important day, they're finally lit —
becoming the brightest thing in the room.

Then everyone finishes singing
and blows them all out.

That's their moment of glory. Also their ending.

But look at it another way —
at least they actually shone.
At least they got to hold someone's wish.
Without them, where does the wish go?
en.editorNote: Say this at a birthday — the person will hesitate for a second before blowing out the candles. The last line about holding wishes can pull the joke back from dark to warm, depending on where you want to leave it.
variations: Candles might be the most optimistic existentialists: fully aware they're burning, fully aware they'll go out, still burning as bright as they can until the last moment — not without a choice, just choosing to shine.

items.sourceNote: 辦公室遺囑印表機，原創

zh-tw.content:
老陳的遺囑上寫著：
「把我的骨灰撒在讓我最快樂的地方。」

三個月後，同事們聚在辦公室的印表機旁邊。

因為老陳生前說過：
「每次卡紙修好的那一瞬間，
是我一天裡感覺最爽的時刻。」

沒有人反對這個決定。

大家都懂。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給你辦公室裡最愛修印表機、或是每次卡紙都淡定修好的那個人——他看了之後一定說「這個我懂」，然後若有所思地看了一眼印表機
variations: 有人說，那是老陳職涯裡唯一一次不用等電話、不用回報告、不用開會、只需要靜靜地在那裡就好的午後——也許真的是他最快樂的地方也說不定。

en.content:
Old Chen's will read:
"Scatter my ashes where I was happiest."

Three months later, his colleagues gathered around the office printer.

Because Chen had always said:
"The moment a paper jam finally clears —
that's the best feeling of my entire day."

Nobody objected to the decision.

Everyone understood.
en.editorNote: Send to the colleague who always calmly fixes the printer — they'll say 'I get it,' then glance at the printer with a thoughtful look
variations: Someone said: that was the only afternoon in Chen's career when he didn't have to take calls, file reports, or sit in meetings. He just stood there and fixed something. Maybe it really was his happiest place.

items.sourceNote: 臨終遺憾台積電，原創

zh-tw.content:
老王臨終前，抓著兒子的手說：
「我這輩子最後悔的，是沒有多陪陪家人。」

兒子眼眶紅了。

老王停頓了一下，繼續說：
「第二後悔的，是當年沒有買台積電。」

兒子：「爸，我們剛才在說——」
老王：「你知道那時候是幾塊嗎……」

家人沉默。
護士也默默離開了房間。
zh-tw.editorNote: 地獄之處在於：很多人的後悔排序，真的就是這樣——而且老王至少知道自己後悔什麼，比不知道的人要誠實一點
variations: 兒子後來說：「如果他真的買了台積電，他的第一後悔一定會換成別的了。」——人就是這樣，後悔清單是無限供應的，只是裡面的順序不停在換。

en.content:
On his deathbed, Old Wang grabbed his son's hand:
"My greatest regret in life is not spending more time with family."

His son's eyes welled up.

Old Wang paused, then continued:
"My second regret is not buying TSMC back in the day."

Son: "Dad, we were just talking about—"
Old Wang: "Do you know what the price was then..."

The family went silent.
The nurse quietly stepped out of the room.
en.editorNote: The dark part: for a lot of people, that really is the order of regrets. And at least Old Wang knew what he regretted — which is more honest than most.
variations: His son said later: "If he'd actually bought it, the number one regret would've been something else anyway." That's how it works — the regret list is infinite. Only the rankings keep changing.

items.sourceNote: 保險業務對話，原創

zh-tw.content:
保險業務員熱情地說：
「我們這個保單，理賠迅速、態度親切——」

客戶打斷他：「我不想買死亡保險。」

業務員：「為什麼？」

「因為用到的時候，我已經不在了。」

業務員：「那是為了您的家人。」

「家人不需要我的死來換錢。」

業務員：「但他們可能需要您離開之後的保障。」

客戶沉默了很久。

最後說：「你繼續說吧。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 地獄在哪？在於業務員說的每一句都是真話，讓人在笑完之後，突然理解了為什麼買保險是個嚴肅的成人決定——是一種對家人的責任，而不是在買自己的死亡
variations: 業務員最後加了一句：「我的佣金讓我孩子這個月有學費交。」客戶：「……我需要你閉嘴，但還是告訴我保額是多少。」

en.content:
The insurance agent spoke enthusiastically:
"Our policy pays out quickly, and our service team is very warm—"

Client interrupted: "I don't want life insurance."

Agent: "Why not?"

"Because when it kicks in, I won't be around anymore."

Agent: "It's for your family."

"My family doesn't need my death to pay for things."

Agent: "But they may need a safety net after you're gone."

The client was quiet for a long time.

Finally: "Go on."
en.editorNote: The dark part: everything the agent says is true. The joke makes you laugh — and then realize why buying life insurance is actually a serious adult act. It's not buying your own death. It's a form of care for the people you'll leave behind.
variations: Agent added: "My commission pays for my kid's school fees this month." Client: "...I need you to stop talking. But tell me the coverage amount."

items.sourceNote: 殭屍等同deadline，原創

zh-tw.content:
「你知道殭屍走那麼慢，為什麼還讓人那麼害怕嗎？」
「為什麼？」
「因為不管你跑多快，它還是一步一步追過來。」
「所以？」
「所以它就是deadline。
你熬夜、你拖延、你祈禱它消失，
你假裝它不存在——
它不生氣、不加速、不跟你講道理，
就是在那個時間點，出現在你面前。」

朋友沉默了一下。
「……你能不能找一個不這麼準確的比喻。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在趕deadline的人，但要等他交完再傳——在最緊張的時候傳這個，可能讓他更絕望。交完之後傳，就是精準的心理按摩
variations: 殭屍和主管的共同點：都在你最不想面對的時候出現，都不接受「再給我一點時間」，都有一種冷靜的可怕。

en.content:
"Do you know why zombies are terrifying even though they move so slowly?"
"Why?"
"Because no matter how fast you run, they're still coming. Step by step."
"So?"
"So they're deadlines.
You stay up all night, procrastinate, pray they'll vanish,
pretend the problem doesn't exist —
they don't get angry, don't speed up, don't argue,
they just show up at exactly the right time."

Friend paused.
"...Could you find an analogy that's a little less accurate?"
en.editorNote: Send to someone chasing a deadline — but wait until they've submitted. Sending this mid-panic could make things worse. After submission, it's the perfect pressure valve.
variations: What zombies and managers have in common: both appear at the moment you least want to face them, both reject 'just a little more time,' and both have a quietly terrifying calm about them.

items.sourceNote: 奶奶不退休，原創

zh-tw.content:
爺爺退休那天，興奮地問奶奶：
「我們今天要去哪裡？」

奶奶說：「哪裡也不去。」

「為什麼？」

「你退休了，但我沒有。
我家裡的工作，從你工作第一天做到現在——
沒有週末、沒有加班費、沒有年終、沒有升遷。
我什麼時候退休？」

爺爺第一次認真聽懂了這個問題。

那是他退休的第一天，
也是他人生中最長的沉默之一。
zh-tw.editorNote: 笑點不高，因為它其實不只是笑話。傳給懂的人，看他笑完能不能靜一下——或是想想身邊有沒有一個「從來沒有退休日」的人
variations: 奶奶說完，繼續進廚房了。爺爺站在原地。這個畫面，比任何退休派對都更讓人思考一生的意義和虧欠。

en.content:
On Grandpa's first day of retirement, he excitedly asked Grandma:
"Where are we going today?"

Grandma: "Nowhere."

"Why?"

"You retired. I didn't.
I've been working this house since your first day on the job —
no weekends, no overtime pay, no bonus, no promotion.
When do I retire?"

Grandpa heard the question clearly for the first time.

It was his first day of retirement.
Also one of the longest silences of his life.
en.editorNote: The laugh line is low — because it's not really just a joke. Send to someone who gets it, and see if they can sit quietly after laughing — or think about whether there's someone in their life who's never had a day off.
variations: Grandma walked back to the kitchen when she was done. Grandpa stood there. That scene says more about a life's debts than any retirement party.

items.sourceNote: 後悔高效系統，原創

zh-tw.content:
人生中效率最高的活動，是後悔。

你在後悔一件已經無法改變的事，
同時，錯過了當下正在發生的事，
讓未來又多了值得後悔的素材。

每一次後悔，都在為下一次後悔鋪路。

這個系統非常穩定，幾乎從不當機，
而且完全免費——無需訂閱，終身使用。
zh-tw.editorNote: 不是在罵你，是在遞鏡子。最好的使用方式：先笑，然後關上手機，去做一直在拖的那件事。或是繼續滑，再後悔一次，讓系統繼續運行
variations: 後悔系統的付費升級版：和別人比較——這樣後悔的效率可以再提升三倍，同時附贈焦慮和失眠。完全免費，且已被廣泛採用。

en.content:
The most efficient activity in a person's life is regret.

You're regretting something that can no longer be changed,
while simultaneously missing what's happening right now,
creating fresh material to regret in the future.

Every regret paves the way for the next one.

This system is remarkably stable, almost never crashes,
and it's completely free — no subscription needed, lifetime access guaranteed.
en.editorNote: Not scolding you — handing you a mirror. Best usage: laugh, then put down your phone and do the thing you've been putting off. Or keep scrolling, regret it once more, and let the system keep running.
variations: The premium upgrade: compare yourself to others — this boosts the regret output by three times and comes bundled with complimentary anxiety and insomnia. Free of charge. Widely adopted.

items.sourceNote: 時間最公平論，原創

zh-tw.content:
「人生最公平的事是什麼？」

「時間。每個人每天都是二十四小時，有錢沒錢都一樣。」

「那有錢人和沒錢的人差在哪？」

「有錢人用錢買時間。
沒錢的人用時間換錢。

公平在哪裡？
在於每個人對『公平』的解釋，
通常取決於他在哪一邊。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 說完可以讓對方決定要怎麼繼續這個話題——通常會進入一個意外深刻的討論，或是大家一起陷入沉默，然後繼續各自的二十四小時
variations: 補充：兩種人都會說「我很忙」，但忙的內容和忙完之後的去向，完全不同。這也叫公平。

en.content:
"What's the fairest thing in life?"

"Time. Everyone gets twenty-four hours a day — rich or poor, exactly the same."

"So what's the difference between people with money and people without?"

"People with money buy time with money.
People without money sell time for money.

Where's the fairness?
In that everyone's definition of 'fair'
usually depends on which side of the equation they're standing on."
en.editorNote: After this, let the other person decide where the conversation goes — it usually becomes surprisingly deep, or everyone goes quiet and returns to their own twenty-four hours.
variations: Addendum: both groups say "I'm so busy," but what they're busy with — and where that busyness leads — are entirely different things. That's also called fairness.

items.sourceNote: 哲學課最後一天，原創

zh-tw.content:
哲學老師問：
「如果你只剩一天可活，你會怎麼過？」

同學們回答：
「陪家人」、「去旅行」、「說出一直想說的話」。

老師說：
「有個前輩說——他那天要繼續來上課。」

同學：「為什麼？」

「因為那一天，時間感覺特別漫長。
他說，這輩子沒有一堂課比那堂課更難熬。」

教室裡沒有人笑。
但大家都懂了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給任何一個正在等某件事結束的人聽——他會突然覺得有點不那麼難熬了，或是更難熬了，兩種都是正常反應
variations: 另一個前輩說：他那天要去開所有他最不想開的會——因為只有在那種時候，時間才真的感覺過不完。

en.content:
The philosophy teacher asked:
"If you only had one day left to live, how would you spend it?"

Students answered:
"With family," "Traveling," "Saying what I've always meant to say."

The teacher said:
"One former student said he'd come to class."

Student: "Why?"

"Because that day, time would feel like it lasts forever.
He said no class had ever been harder to get through than that one would be."

Nobody in the room laughed.
But everyone understood.
en.editorNote: Tell this to anyone who's waiting for something painful to be over — they'll either feel slightly less miserable, or slightly more. Both are valid responses.
variations: Another former student said he'd go to every meeting he'd been dreading — because those were the only times time genuinely refused to move.

items.sourceNote: 面試發展空間諷刺，原創

zh-tw.content:
面試官說：「這個職位發展空間非常大。」

求職者：「能說詳細一點嗎？」

面試官：「你現在坐的那個位子，
已經有三個人坐過了。
每個人都在這裡待了一段時間，
然後去了更好的地方。」

求職者：「那我也可以嗎？」

面試官：「幾乎都可以。
我們這裡的流動率非常穩定。」

求職者心想：
這算鼓勵還是警告？
zh-tw.editorNote: 面試前傳給準備去面試的朋友，當成一個緊張氣氛的緩解器——也是一個提醒：記得問清楚「更好的地方」，是主動離開還是被迫的
variations: 面試官補充：「而且我們的離職流程非常完善，從第一天就開始培訓了。」——求職者決定這句話不需要深究。

en.content:
Interviewer: "This position has enormous growth potential."

Applicant: "Can you be more specific?"

Interviewer: "The seat you're sitting in right now —
three people have sat there before you.
Each one stayed for a while,
then moved on to better things."

Applicant: "So I can too?"

Interviewer: "Almost certainly.
Our turnover rate here is very consistent."

Applicant thought:
Is this encouragement or a warning?
en.editorNote: Send to a friend before their interview as a tension-breaker — and a reminder to ask whether 'moved on to better things' was voluntary or otherwise.
variations: Interviewer added: "We also have a very thorough offboarding process. We start preparing for it from day one." The applicant decided not to unpack that sentence.

items.sourceNote: 詛咒式安慰，原創

zh-tw.content:
「你知道嗎，你其實比很多人都幸運。」

「哪裡幸運？」

「你知道自己的問題是什麼。
很多人連問題在哪都搞不清楚，
所以連怎麼開始解決都不知道。
你已經贏在起跑點了。」

「但我知道問題、卻解決不了。」

「那是第二個幸運——
你還有很大的改善空間，
代表旅程還沒結束。」

「……謝謝你的安慰。」

「不客氣。你的第三個幸運是認識了我。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在困境中、需要一點黑色幽默才能繼續撐下去的朋友——真正的好朋友就是那個能把「你的情況很糟糕」說成「你比很多人幸運」的人，而且你還無法反駁
variations: 這種安慰法的優點是：對方笑完了，還是會覺得你說的有幾分道理——因為你說的確實是真的，只是從一個奇怪的角度說出來了。

en.content:
"You know what? You're luckier than most people."

"How am I lucky?"

"You know what your problem is.
Most people can't even locate their problem,
so they have no idea where to start.
You're already ahead."

"But knowing what it is doesn't mean I can fix it."

"That's your second piece of luck —
you still have enormous room to improve,
which means the story isn't over."

"...Thank you for the comfort."

"Don't mention it. Your third piece of luck is knowing me."
en.editorNote: Send to a friend who's struggling and needs a dose of dark humor to keep going — a real friend is someone who can frame 'your situation is bad' as 'you're actually lucky' and make it impossible to argue with.
variations: The advantage of this kind of comfort: they laugh, and then realize you actually had a point. Because you did. You just delivered it from a very strange angle.

items.sourceNote: 下禮拜一的永恆等待，原創

zh-tw.content:
「我打算下禮拜一開始減肥。」

「今天不就是禮拜一嗎？」

「所以還有七天。」

「……你說的是下下禮拜一？」

「我說的是下一個真正適合開始的禮拜一。
那個禮拜一和現在這個會不一樣的，
我感覺得出來。」

朋友沉默了一下。
「我的健身房會費，已經繳了三年了。」
「你去了嗎？」
「我去簽約的時候去了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每個說「下禮拜一開始」的人，包括你自己——最地獄的地方是健身房那句，因為每個人都有自己版本的「只有在簽約時去過」
variations: 「那個真正的禮拜一」和「明天我一定早起」、「今年一定要存到錢」，是人類史上最充滿希望也最難實現的三個承諾，並列第一。

en.content:
"I'm going to start my diet next Monday."

"Isn't today Monday?"

"So I have seven more days."

"...Are you talking about the Monday after next?"

"I'm talking about the next Monday that genuinely feels right.
That one will be different from this one.
I can sense it."

Friend paused.
"I've been paying for my gym membership for three years."
"Have you been going?"
"I went when I signed up."
en.editorNote: Send to everyone who says 'starting next Monday' — including yourself. The darkest part is the gym line, because everyone has their own version of 'I only went for the signup.'
variations: 'The Monday that genuinely feels right,' 'I'll definitely wake up early tomorrow,' and 'I'll finally save money this year' — humanity's three most hopeful and least fulfilled promises, tied for first place.

items.sourceNote: 人生最快最慢，原創

zh-tw.content:
問：人生中什麼跑得最快？

答：錢。
月初進來，月底消失，
你連它什麼時候走的都沒注意到。

問：那什麼最慢？

答：時間——
但只在你等一件很痛苦的事結束的時候。
其他時候，它快得讓你來不及說再見。

問：那什麼最公平？

答：以上兩點，同時存在。
zh-tw.editorNote: 深夜傳給月底快撐不住的朋友，或是正在等某件事結束的人——它能讓人笑，也能讓人靜下來想一下，兩種都有效
variations: 對時間感知最準確的不是老人，是正在還房貸的年輕人，以及快要考完試的學生——只有他們真正知道一分鐘可以有多長。

en.content:
Question: What moves the fastest in life?

Answer: Money.
It arrives at the start of the month and disappears by the end,
and you don't even notice it leaving.

Question: And the slowest?

Answer: Time —
but only when you're waiting for something painful to end.
Every other moment, it moves so fast you can't say goodbye.

Question: And what's the fairest?

Answer: That both of those things are true at the same time.
en.editorNote: Send late at night to the friend barely making it through the end of the month, or to someone waiting for something hard to pass — it makes people laugh and then quietly think, and both responses are the right ones.
variations: The most accurate judges of time aren't old people — they're young adults paying off a mortgage, and students with five minutes left on an exam. Only they truly know how long a minute can last.

items.sourceNote: 假期最後一晚，原創

zh-tw.content:
「你知道一年裡最快樂的是哪一天嗎？」

「哪一天？」

「假期開始的第一天。」

「那最悲劇的呢？」

「假期結束前的最後一個晚上。
你沒做完任何原本計劃的事，
不知道時間去哪了，
只知道明天要回去了。

就在這一刻，
你終於感受到一種強烈的——對假期的珍惜。

在剩下五小時的時候。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 假期結束前一晚傳給還在滑手機的朋友，讓他在感同身受中度過最後幾小時——這也是最準確的人生縮影，不只是假期的事
variations: 人生版：最後悔的往往不是沒做什麼，是在快要來不及的時候，才開始真正在乎做了什麼。這跟假期最後一晚的感覺，一模一樣。

en.content:
"Do you know what the happiest day of the year is?"

"What?"

"The first day of your vacation."

"And the most tragic?"

"The last night before it ends.
You haven't done any of the things you planned.
You don't know where the time went.
You only know you have to go back tomorrow.

At exactly that moment,
you finally feel an intense — appreciation for the vacation.

With five hours left."
en.editorNote: Send this to the friend still scrolling their phone the last night of a holiday — let them spend their remaining hours in full recognition of this moment. It's also the most accurate summary of life, and not just about vacations.
variations: Life version: the deepest regrets aren't usually about what we didn't do — they're about not realizing we cared until there was almost no time left. The same feeling as the last night of a vacation. Every time.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「醫生說你還有幾個月可活，但帳單先到」的黑色幽默醫療笑話結構，改寫重製

zh-tw.content:
醫生嚴肅地說：
「檢查結果出來了，你大概只剩三個月。」

病患沉默了很久。

「那我能做什麼？」

「活好每一天，放下煩惱，珍惜身邊的人。」

病患點了點頭。

兩個禮拜後，醫療帳單寄到了。
金額足以讓人再活個五年。

病患打電話給醫院：
「可以分二十四期嗎？」

客服說：「當然，我們非常彈性。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 最地獄的部分不是病，是帳單。傳給剛繳完醫療費的朋友，他會笑，然後嘆一口氣——因為那個客服說「非常彈性」的語氣他太熟悉了
variations: 病患後來說：「分期付款反而是讓我繼續活著最強的動力。我得活到還完才能走。」

en.content:
The doctor said gravely:
"The results are in. You probably have about three months left."

The patient was silent for a long time.

"What should I do?"

"Live each day fully. Let go of worries. Cherish the people around you."

The patient nodded.

Two weeks later, the medical bill arrived.
Enough to fund five more years of living.

The patient called the hospital:
"Can I do twenty-four monthly installments?"

Customer service: "Of course. We're very flexible."
en.editorNote: The darkest part isn't the diagnosis — it's the bill. Send to a friend who just paid a medical expense; they'll laugh and then sigh, because that cheerful 'very flexible' tone is all too familiar.
variations: The patient later said: "The installment plan is actually the strongest motivation I have to keep living. I need to survive long enough to pay it off."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「阿茲海默症患者的意外好消息」式幽默，重新建構場景與對白

zh-tw.content:
醫生說：「我有好消息和壞消息。」

「壞消息是什麼？」

「你有輕微的記憶衰退，
可能會越來越記不住最近發生的事。」

老張沉默了。
「那……好消息是？」

「好消息是——
你也會越來越記不住最近發生的事。」

老張皺眉：「這不是同一件事嗎？」

醫生：「對。
但你等一下就會忘記我說過壞消息了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給那個最近壓力很大、腦子塞太多東西的人聽——先笑，然後偷偷希望自己也有一個可以選擇性失憶的功能，只刪除壞的記憶
variations: 老張回家後告訴老伴：「醫生說我記性不好。」老伴說：「他上個月就說過了。」老張：「……真的嗎？那至少他的診斷很穩定。」

en.content:
The doctor said: "I have good news and bad news."

"What's the bad news?"

"You have mild memory loss.
You'll gradually stop retaining recent events."

Old Chang went quiet.
"And... the good news?"

"The good news is —
you'll also gradually stop retaining recent events."

Old Chang frowned: "Isn't that the same thing?"

Doctor: "Yes.
But in a moment, you'll have forgotten I ever mentioned the bad news."
en.editorNote: Tell this to someone who's been overwhelmed and overstuffed with stress — they'll laugh, then quietly wish they had selective memory that could delete the bad parts.
variations: Old Chang went home and told his wife: "The doctor says my memory is going." She said: "He said the same thing last month." Chang: "...Did he? Well, at least his diagnosis is consistent."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自墓誌銘幽默與臨終遺言諷刺類笑話，場景與人物完全原創

zh-tw.content:
小明生前交代：
「我的墓碑上，就刻那句我說了一輩子的話。」

家人以為是什麼人生格言，
或是對家人的感謝。

墓碑刻好，大家站在那裡看。

上面寫著：

「等一下，我快好了。」

沉默了很久。

他大兒子說：「……爸，你現在終於好了嗎？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個做什麼事都說「等一下」、永遠在最後一刻才就緒的人——他會笑到停不下來，然後說「這就是我的墓誌銘沒錯了」
variations: 小明的朋友說：「我們等了他六十年了，沒想到連走的時候他也沒忘記說這句話。這才是真正的始終如一。」

en.content:
Before he died, Xiao Ming left instructions:
"Just carve the phrase I said my whole life onto my tombstone."

The family assumed it would be a life motto,
or a message of gratitude to loved ones.

The stone was finished. Everyone stood there reading it.

It said:

"Hold on, I'm almost ready."

A long silence.

His oldest son said: "...Dad. Are you finally ready now?"
en.editorNote: Send to the person who says 'hold on, almost done' before everything, always finishing at the last possible moment — they'll laugh until they can't stop, then say 'yeah, that's my epitaph.'
variations: His friend said: "We waited sixty years for him. Turns out he didn't forget to say it even at the end. That's what you call true consistency."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「遺願清單拖延」與人生倒數計時幽默，場景全部原創重寫

zh-tw.content:
老李退休的時候，
掏出一張皺巴巴的紙說：
「這是我的人生清單，
我終於有時間去完成了。」

兒子接過來一看。

清單上第一條：「整理這張清單。」

老李解釋：「這樣我才知道後面要做什麼。」

兒子往下看。

第二條：「想清楚第三條要寫什麼。」

沉默。

「爸，這張清單你寫了多久了？」

老李想了一下：「大概……三十年？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給說「等我退休了就要做XXX」的人——最地獄的真相是：人生清單不是缺少時間，是缺少那個「先整理一下清單」的勇氣去正式開始
variations: 老李後來說：「其實我知道第一件事是什麼。我只是怕一旦開始做，就代表承認之前沒在做。」——這大概是所有拖延的真正原因。

en.content:
When Old Li retired,
he pulled out a crumpled piece of paper and said:
"This is my life list.
I finally have time to work through it."

His son took it and looked.

Item one: "Organize this list."

Old Li explained: "So I know what comes next."

His son read on.

Item two: "Figure out what to write for item three."

Silence.

"Dad, how long have you been working on this list?"

Old Li thought for a moment: "About... thirty years?"
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who says 'when I retire, I'll finally do X' — the dark truth is that a life list doesn't need more time. It needs the courage to officially begin, which means admitting you haven't started yet.
variations: Old Li said later: "I actually know what the first item is. I just didn't want to start — because starting means admitting I hadn't been doing it all along." That's probably the real reason behind all procrastination.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「在表演中斷氣的喜劇演員——至少他殺紅了」式黑色幽默，完全改寫成中文場景

zh-tw.content:
阿文說：「如果我有一天是在舞台上表演時走的，
我覺得那是最完美的結局。」

朋友：「為什麼？」

「因為大家會說：
『他今晚笑死人了。』
然後停頓一下——
然後意識到這句話是字面意思。」

朋友沉默了一秒。

「……你是在認真計劃這個嗎？」

阿文：「不是計劃，是期待。
人生難得有一個結尾可以讓人回味。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給那個熱愛表演、或是把「讓人笑」當成人生使命的朋友聽——他會笑完，然後認真思考一下，最後說「老實說，這也是我想要的」
variations: 另一個版本：阿文說，最怕的其實不是那樣走，是走了之後，台下的人還在等他繼續講下一個笑話——因為他的表情看起來就是在蓄力。

en.content:
A-Wen said: "If I ever go while performing on stage,
I think that would be the perfect ending."

Friend: "Why?"

"Because everyone will say:
'He killed it tonight.'
And then pause —
and then realize that sentence is literally true."

Friend was quiet for a second.

"...Are you actually planning this?"

A-Wen: "Not planning. Hoping.
It's rare to find an ending in life
that people actually remember."
en.editorNote: Tell this to the friend who lives for performing, or whose life mission is to make people laugh — they'll laugh first, think quietly for a moment, then say 'honestly, that's the one I'd want too.'
variations: Another version: A-Wen said what he actually feared wasn't going out that way — it was the audience still waiting for the next punchline afterward, because his expression would look exactly like he was building up to something.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自2026年流行的「被AI取代」企業虛無主義幽默，場景與對白完全原創重寫

zh-tw.content:
公司宣布：「我們導入AI，是為了讓員工更有效率、更有創意。」

三個月後，公司又宣布：
「感謝各位的貢獻，我們決定精簡人力。」

小江收到通知，靜靜地看了很久。

然後他打開電腦，輸入：
「請幫我寫一封關於被AI取代的心情的離職感言。」

AI回覆：「沒問題，我很樂意幫忙。」

小江盯著螢幕。

「……連這個你也比我快。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給那個剛被裁員、或是每天活在「下一個被取代的是我嗎」焦慮裡的朋友——他會先笑，然後說一句「這根本不是笑話」，然後繼續笑
variations: 小江後來說：「我唯一的優勢，是我不需要插電。」AI沉默了一下。他覺得那個停頓很像在思考。

en.content:
The company announced: "We're adopting AI to help employees work more efficiently and creatively."

Three months later, the company announced:
"Thank you all for your contributions. We've decided to streamline our workforce."

Xiao Jiang read the notice quietly for a long time.

Then he opened his laptop and typed:
"Please write a farewell message about how it feels to be replaced by AI."

The AI replied: "Sure, happy to help."

Xiao Jiang stared at the screen.

"...You're faster at this too."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just got laid off, or who lives daily with the anxiety of 'am I next?' — they'll laugh first, then say 'this isn't even a joke,' then keep laughing.
variations: Xiao Jiang said later: "My only advantage is I don't need to be plugged in." The AI paused for a moment. He thought it felt a little too much like thinking.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「我不怕死，我怕葬禮上的閒聊」式幽默，場景與人物全部原創

zh-tw.content:
有人問老謝：「你怕死嗎？」

老謝想了一下：「不怕。」

「真的不怕？」

「真的。我怕的是葬禮。」

「為什麼？」

「因為葬禮上一定有人走過來說：
『最近還好嗎？好久不見耶。』

而且他問的是我，不是亡者。

死了都還要應付社交，
這才是真正的恐怖。」

朋友想了一下。

「那你要不要把你的葬禮辦成謝絕出席？」

老謝：「有這個選項嗎？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個一聽到「聚會」就頭痛、或是每次參加葬禮回來都說「最累的不是難過，是一直被問近況」的人——這個笑話會讓他覺得被理解了
variations: 老謝後來說：「其實我最擔心的是，有人會趁機在靈堂上找我聊房地產投資。他平常就這樣。死了也逃不掉。」

en.content:
Someone asked Old Xie: "Are you afraid of dying?"

Old Xie thought for a second. "No."

"Really?"

"Really. What I'm afraid of is the funeral."

"Why?"

"Because someone will always walk up and say:
'How've you been? It's been so long!'

And they're talking to me, not the deceased.

Having to make small talk even after death —
that's the real nightmare."

Friend thought about it.

"Should you just make yours invitation-only?"

Old Xie: "Is that an option?"
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who dreads every gathering, or who comes back from every funeral saying 'the exhausting part wasn't the grief, it was the small talk' — this joke will make them feel genuinely seen.
variations: Old Xie said later: "What I really worry about is someone using the wake as a chance to pitch me on a real estate investment. He does it everywhere. Death won't stop him."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「黑色幽默的臨終遺言剖析」，場景與對白完全原創，與dh-018墓誌銘方向不同，側重職場工作狂

zh-tw.content:
老張人生最後清醒的時刻，
家人圍在床邊，屏氣凝神等著。

老張緩緩張開嘴：

「我的……工作信箱……密碼是……」

大家眼淚都出來了。

「……記得幫我……交接……」

兒子哽咽：「爸，有什麼想說的嗎？」

老張：「……這個月報表還沒跑完。」

然後闔上眼睛。

安靜了很久。

最後兒子說：「……至少他走的時候，
心裡想的還是沒讓公司失望。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個把工作看得比命還重的朋友，或是下班了還在查信箱的人——他會笑，然後沉默，然後把手機放下來一秒鐘，再拿起來
variations: 老張的同事後來說：「他那個月的報表，我們替他跑完了。數字很漂亮。」沒有人知道這算不算一種告別的圓滿。

en.content:
In Old Zhang's last lucid moments,
family gathered at the bedside, holding their breath.

Old Zhang slowly opened his mouth:

"My work email... the password is..."

Tears came to everyone's eyes.

"...Make sure you... hand things off..."

His son choked up: "Dad, is there anything you want to say?"

Old Zhang: "...This month's report... isn't finished."

Then his eyes closed.

A long silence.

Finally his son said: "...At least in his last moment,
he was thinking about not letting the company down."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who treats work like it's more important than life, or the one still checking emails after midnight — they'll laugh, go quiet, put down their phone for one second, then pick it up again.
variations: His colleague said later: "We finished that month's report for him. The numbers looked good." Nobody knew if that counted as a kind of closure.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「人生就是笑話，死亡是梗底」以及「長大就好了的謊言」式黑色幽默，原創場景

zh-tw.content:
小時候大人說：「長大就好了。」

你長大了。

你有了工作、有了帳單、有了保險、
有了一個裝滿「等我有空再做」的清單，
還有一個月底永遠見底的帳戶。

你打電話回家說：「你說長大就好了。」

媽媽停頓了一下。

「我的意思是……
你不用再擔心我幫你決定。

現在換你自己擔心了。」

電話那頭，你能感受到她在忍笑。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給任何一個覺得「大人好難當」的人，最好是在他剛繳完一堆帳單、或是剛開完無聊的會之後——他會笑，然後說「這就是背刺」
variations: 最後你明白了：「長大就好了」的意思，其實是「長大就輪到你煩了」——只是沒有人在你小的時候跟你說清楚，因為說清楚了你不會長大。

en.content:
When you were small, the adults said: "It'll be better when you grow up."

So you grew up.

Now you have a job, bills, insurance,
a list full of "I'll get to it when I have time,"
and a bank account that empties itself every month.

You call home: "You said it would be better when I grew up."

Your mom pauses.

"What I meant was...
you wouldn't have to worry about me deciding things for you.

Now you get to worry about it yourself."

Through the phone, you can tell she's trying not to laugh.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who finds adulting genuinely hard — best right after they've paid a stack of bills or survived a pointless meeting. They'll laugh and say 'this is a betrayal.'
variations: Eventually you understand: 'it'll be better when you grow up' really meant 'it'll be your problem when you grow up.' Nobody told you that clearly when you were small — because if they had, you might not have bothered.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「黑色幽默是我的心理治療」文章中關於正向思考處方的荒謬反差，場景與對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
心理師說：「你這個月怎麼樣？」

小涵：「不太好。一直負面思考。」

心理師點點頭：「那我們來練習正向思考。
試著找出每件事好的一面。」

小涵沉默了一下。

「好。那我正向思考看看——
我這個月的存款快見底了，
好的一面是：
我不需要擔心要放在哪裡了。」

心理師停了很久。

「我的意思不完全是這個。」

「但你說每件事都有好的一面。」

「……你學得很快。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個被人叫去「想開一點」、然後真的認真想、卻得出奇怪結論的朋友——有時候負面思考只是比較誠實，不代表需要被修正
variations: 小涵後來說：「我現在理解正向思考了。它的意思是：用一個不同的角度讓自己騙過自己。這其實挺需要創意的。」

en.content:
The therapist asked: "How have you been this month?"

Xiao Han: "Not great. A lot of negative thinking."

The therapist nodded. "Let's practice positive thinking.
Try to find the bright side of each situation."

Xiao Han paused.

"Okay. Let me try —
My savings are almost gone this month.
Bright side:
I don't have to worry about where to put it anymore."

The therapist was quiet for a while.

"That's not quite what I meant."

"But you said every situation has a bright side."

"...You learn fast."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who was told to 'look on the bright side,' actually tried it sincerely, and arrived at a strange conclusion — sometimes negative thinking is just more honest, not something that needs fixing.
variations: Xiao Han said later: "I get positive thinking now. It means: trick yourself with a different angle. That actually takes a lot of creativity."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自現代人用社群媒體處理悲傷的荒謬現象，以LINE群組弔唁場景呈現，場景完全原創

zh-tw.content:
老同學群組傳來消息：
「大家，志明走了。」

群組安靜了三秒。

然後：

「🙏」
「🙏🙏」
「這麼突然……」
「🙏🙏🙏」
「節哀順變」
「什麼時候的事？」
「🙏」
「這樣啊……」

然後群組又沉默了。

過了十分鐘，有人傳：
「對了，週六烤肉還去嗎？」

沒有人覺得這個問題問得不對。

人生就是這樣，繼續的。
zh-tw.editorNote: 不是在說那個問烤肉的人，是在說那種感覺——悲傷、茫然、然後生活繼續——群組裡的沉默和那個問題，其實都是同一種不知道怎麼辦的表現
variations: 志明看到的話可能會說：「週六我就不去了。」然後又覺得這句話可以當成一個還不錯的告別。

en.content:
A message appeared in the old classmates' group chat:
"Everyone — Zhiming is gone."

The group went silent for three seconds.

Then:

"🙏"
"🙏🙏"
"So sudden..."
"🙏🙏🙏"
"My condolences"
"When did it happen?"
"🙏"
"Oh no..."

Then the group went quiet again.

Ten minutes later, someone typed:
"Hey, are we still doing the BBQ on Saturday?"

Nobody thought it was the wrong question to ask.

Life is like that. It keeps going.
en.editorNote: It's not about the person who asked about the BBQ — it's about that feeling. Grief, disorientation, and then life resuming. The silence and that question are both just different ways of not knowing what to do.
variations: If Zhiming could see it, he might have said: "Count me out for Saturday." And then thought that wasn't actually a bad way to say goodbye.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「瀕死經驗改變人生觀」的諷刺反差——存活之後又回到一模一樣的日子，場景完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿誠出車禍，在急診室清醒過來。

他說：「我以為我要走了。
那一刻，我腦海中閃過的，
是我這輩子還沒做的事——
沒去旅行、沒說出口的話、
沒陪夠家人的時間。」

朋友感動地說：「那現在呢？
你要改變生活了嗎？」

阿誠看了一下手機。

「先回覆一下主管的訊息。
他說昨天的報告有個數字要改。」

朋友沉默。

「我說，要改變了嗎？」

「我知道。下週。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 瀕死經驗的地獄之處不是死亡，是活回來之後的那個週一——沉默片刻，然後繼續。傳給那個說「等一個契機就改變」的人
variations: 阿誠後來真的去旅行了。他說：「我在機場回覆了四十三封工作信件。但我有去機場，這樣也算。」

en.content:
A-Cheng woke up in the emergency room after a car accident.

He said: "I thought I was done.
In that moment, everything I hadn't done flashed through my mind —
trips never taken, things never said,
time never given to the people I love."

His friend said, moved: "So what now?
Are you going to change your life?"

A-Cheng looked at his phone.

"Let me reply to my manager first.
He says there's a number to fix in yesterday's report."

Friend went silent.

"I said — are you going to change?"

"I know. Next week."
en.editorNote: The dark part of a near-death experience isn't the almost-dying — it's the Monday you come back to. A quiet pause, and then: continue. Send to anyone waiting for a 'sign' to make a change.
variations: A-Cheng did eventually travel. He said: "I replied to forty-three work emails at the airport. But I was at the airport. That counts for something."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自2026年「自我提升文化與生產力崇拜」的諷刺評論，以勵志演講場景呈現，對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
講師在台上激動地說：
「你們知道成功人士和普通人的差別在哪嗎？
就是那五分鐘！
每天早起五分鐘，
你的人生就會完全不同！」

台下的小美認真地舉手：
「那如果我已經在五點起床了？」

講師：「那就四點五十五！
早起永遠有意義！」

小美：「……那我晚上幾點睡？」

講師微笑：「成功的人，
不把時間花在睡眠上。」

小美低頭看了一眼門票金額。

「我付了一千二百塊，
學到的是可以不用睡覺。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給任何一個買過成功學課程、聽完一堆「早起改變人生」回家繼續睡的人——那一千二百塊換來的，是一個非常扎實的睡前故事
variations: 小美後來說：「我決定用那個早起的五分鐘，複習一下我為什麼花了一千二百塊來聽這件事。答案每天都不一樣。」

en.content:
The speaker on stage said passionately:
"Do you know the difference between successful people and ordinary people?
Five minutes!
Wake up five minutes earlier every day
and your life will be completely transformed!"

Xiao Mei in the audience raised her hand:
"What if I'm already waking up at 5am?"

Speaker: "Then 4:55!
There is always meaning in waking up earlier!"

Xiao Mei: "...What time do I go to sleep?"

Speaker, smiling: "Successful people
don't waste time on sleep."

Xiao Mei looked down at her ticket price.

"I paid twelve hundred dollars
to learn I don't need to sleep."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who's ever bought a self-improvement course, sat through an 'early rising changes your life' talk, and went home and slept in anyway — that twelve hundred dollars bought a very solid bedtime story.
variations: Xiao Mei said later: "I've decided to use those five early minutes to think about why I paid twelve hundred dollars to hear this. The answer is different every day."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「遺產爭奪讓家人相聚」諷刺結構，場景人物全部原創重寫，轉為台灣家庭語境

zh-tw.content:
老爸走了之後，三兄弟十年沒聯絡，
終於在同一個屋簷下吃了一頓飯。

大哥說：「我們不應該因為這種事才聚在一起。」

二哥說：「對，太久沒見了。」

三弟說：「爸如果知道，一定很欣慰。」

大家沉默地夾了一口菜。

律師咳了一聲，打開文件。

三個人同時坐直了。

老媽在旁邊說：
「他知道。這就是他為什麼要等到現在才走的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個只有在婚喪喜慶才能讓全家人集合的人——笑點和淚點之間，只差一口菜的距離
variations: 老媽後來說：「他生前說，他最後一件事，就是讓你們三個再坐在一起吃一頓飯。遺產只是理由，不是目的。」三個人都沒說話。飯繼續吃。

en.content:
After their father passed, three brothers who hadn't spoken in ten years
finally sat under the same roof for a meal.

The eldest said: "We shouldn't need something like this to bring us together."

The second said: "Right. It's been way too long."

The youngest said: "Dad would be so happy seeing this."

Everyone quietly reached for a bite of food.

The lawyer cleared his throat and opened the documents.

All three sat up straight at the same time.

Their mother, from the corner, said:
"He knows. That's exactly why he waited until now."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose family only fully assembles for weddings and funerals — the line between laughing and tearing up is only one bite of food apart.
variations: Mom said later: "He told me before he went — his last wish was just to get the three of you eating together one more time. The inheritance was the excuse, not the point." Nobody said anything. They kept eating.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「健康飲食vs壽命」的反差幽默，以營養師諮詢場景呈現，人物對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
營養師說：「你現在的飲食習慣，
讓你未來十年的健康風險大幅提高。」

阿偉點點頭：「那如果我改變呢？」

「戒糖、戒油炸、多蔬果、規律作息——
你的壽命可能延長十年。」

阿偉想了一下。

「那多出來的十年，要做什麼？」

營養師停頓了一下。

「……繼續維持健康的生活習慣。」

阿偉：「所以我努力活得更長，
是為了更努力地活著？」

營養師第一次覺得
這個問題她沒有辦法用衛教手冊回答。
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給正在猶豫要不要「開始健康生活」的人——笑完之後，他可能反而決定去跑個步了，但原因會跟營養師說的完全不一樣
variations: 阿偉最後說：「好，我決定了。我要維持現在的生活，但我要更用力地享受它。」營養師不確定這算不算諮詢成功。

en.content:
The nutritionist said: "Your current eating habits
are significantly raising your health risks over the next ten years."

A-Wei nodded. "What if I change?"

"Cut sugar, no fried food, more vegetables, regular sleep —
you could add ten years to your life."

A-Wei thought for a moment.

"What would I do with those extra ten years?"

The nutritionist paused.

"...Keep maintaining healthy habits."

A-Wei: "So I work hard to live longer
so I can work harder at living?"

It was the first time the nutritionist realized
that question had no answer in any of her pamphlets.
en.editorNote: Tell this to anyone on the fence about 'starting a healthy lifestyle' — after laughing, they might actually go for a run, but for completely different reasons than anything the nutritionist suggested.
variations: A-Wei finally said: "Okay, I've decided. I'm keeping my current lifestyle — but I'm going to enjoy it a lot harder." The nutritionist wasn't sure if the session counted as a success.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「交友軟體誠實個人簡介」梗，結合現代人對自我描述的荒謬落差，場景與文字完全原創

zh-tw.content:
小敏在交友軟體上，決定寫一個「完全誠實」的個人簡介。

她寫道：
「28歲。日夜顛倒。
不確定自己喜歡什麼，
但很確定自己不喜歡什麼。
存款勉強三位數。
對貓的感情比對人穩定。
偶爾覺得人生很美好，
偶爾不這樣覺得。
正在努力，但方向還沒確定。」

她的朋友說：「這樣不會有人配對吧？」

小敏說：「配到的，才是真的懂我。」

她的收件匣，那週爆炸了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個覺得「誠實就不會有人喜歡」的朋友——有時候，最真實的樣子反而是最少競爭的。或者說，找到懂的人，本來就不需要很多人
variations: 小敏後來說：「那些配對我的人，大概也寫過一樣誠實的簡介，只是刪掉了。」

en.content:
Xiao Min decided to write a completely honest dating profile.

She wrote:
"28. Sleep schedule: non-existent.
Not sure what I want,
but very sure what I don't.
Savings barely in the triple digits.
My feelings for cats are more consistent than for people.
Sometimes life feels beautiful.
Sometimes it doesn't.
I'm trying. Direction TBD."

Her friend said: "Nobody's going to match with that."

Xiao Min said: "The ones who do will actually get me."

Her inbox exploded that week.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who thinks 'being honest means nobody will like you' — sometimes the most unfiltered version of yourself has the least competition. Or put another way: finding someone who gets it never required a crowd.
variations: Xiao Min said later: "The people who matched with me probably wrote something equally honest — and then deleted it."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「遺願清單倒著完成」的荒謬反差概念，搭配台灣日常生活細節，場景人物完全原創

zh-tw.content:
老陳八十歲，從床頭拿出一張紙。

「這是我的遺願清單。
最後這幾年，
我把它從後面開始做。」

兒女圍過去看。

清單最後一條：「睡到自然醒。」
已打勾。

倒數第二條：「不用管血壓。」
也打勾了。

倒數第三條：「不去參加沒意義的聚會。」
打勾，還有備注：「從六十五歲就開始執行。」

兒子問：「那第一條是什麼？」

老陳翻到最前面：
「環遊世界。」

沉默了一下。

「這個，你們幫我完成吧。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個遺願清單第一條寫了「環遊世界」、到現在還沒動的人——老陳的智慧在於：先做你真的做得到的，剩下的交出去，也是一種完成
variations: 老陳補充：「其實第一條我本來也有機會。只是當時覺得還有時間。你們懂這個感覺嗎？」大家點頭。沒有一個人說話。

en.content:
Old Chen, at eighty, pulled a piece of paper from his bedside.

"This is my bucket list.
The last few years,
I've been working through it from the bottom up."

His children gathered to look.

Last item: "Sleep as late as I want."
Checked off.

Second to last: "Stop monitoring my blood pressure."
Also checked.

Third from the bottom: "Stop going to pointless social gatherings."
Checked, with a note: "Started enforcing this at 65."

His son asked: "What's the first item?"

Old Chen flipped to the front:
"Travel the world."

A pause.

"That one — I'll leave to you."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone whose bucket list starts with 'travel the world' and hasn't been touched — Old Chen's wisdom: do the things you actually can, and hand off the rest. That's also a way of finishing.
variations: Old Chen added: "I did have a chance at the first one, you know. I just kept thinking there was more time. You understand that feeling, don't you?" Everyone nodded. Nobody spoke.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「冥想app與焦慮的諷刺」概念，以手機通知打斷靜心的場景呈現，對白與細節完全原創

zh-tw.content:
小豪下載了冥想app。

第一天，app通知：
「記得今天冥想十分鐘，放鬆你的大腦。」

第二天：「你已經連續兩天了！繼續保持！」

第五天：「你的連續天數快斷了，快去冥想！」

第七天：「你已經連續七天！你是冥想達人！繼續衝！」

第十天，小豪盯著那個通知，
感覺到一種從未有過的焦慮。

他打開app。

輸入意見回饋：
「你的通知讓我需要冥想才能繼續使用你的冥想app。」

系統回覆：
「謝謝您的回饋！記得今天冥想喔！」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個下載了各種提升自我的app、結果被那些app搞得更焦慮的朋友——工具變成了壓力源，這才是現代人最精準的諷刺之一
variations: 小豪最後刪掉了app。他說：「我現在的冥想方式，是不去想那個連續天數。這大概才是真正的放鬆。」

en.content:
Xiao Hao downloaded a meditation app.

Day one, the app notified him:
"Remember to meditate for ten minutes today. Give your brain a rest."

Day two: "You've meditated two days in a row! Keep it up!"

Day five: "Your streak is about to break — go meditate now!"

Day seven: "Seven-day streak! You're a meditation master! Keep pushing!"

On day ten, Xiao Hao stared at the notification
and felt a new kind of anxiety he'd never had before.

He opened the app.

He typed in the feedback box:
"Your notifications have made me need to meditate
just to keep using your meditation app."

The system replied:
"Thanks for your feedback! Don't forget to meditate today!"
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who downloaded every self-improvement app and ended up more stressed than before — when the tool becomes the source of pressure, that's one of the most accurate satirical portraits of modern life.
variations: Xiao Hao eventually deleted the app. He said: "My meditation practice now is: not thinking about the streak. That's probably what relaxing actually feels like."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自2026年全球租屋危機報導，以看房場景呈現現代人對居住成本的黑色笑話，對白與情節完全原創

zh-tw.content:
房仲帶小林看了一間套房，
興奮地說：
「這裡採光很好，
廚房和臥室合而為一——」

小林看了一下四周。

「廚房和臥室合而為一，
還是廚房就是臥室就是客廳？」

「我們叫它『複合式生活空間』。」

「多少錢？」

房仲報了個數字。

小林沉默了一下。

「我能不能只租浴室？
我在那裡生活的時間夠長了，
感覺比較實際。」

房仲：「浴室目前沒有單獨出租，
但如果你有興趣，閣樓還有一個空間。」

「閣樓是什麼意思？」

「就是你抬頭三十公分就碰到天花板的那種。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在租屋的人，最好是在他剛看完第五間「複合式生活空間」回來之後——他會笑，但笑聲裡有一點心酸，因為這根本不是笑話
variations: 小林後來說：「我決定了，我要住在辦公室。反正我在那裡的時間比家還多，至少不用付房租。」——這句話一半是笑話，一半是認真計劃。

en.content:
The agent showed Xiao Lin a studio apartment and announced cheerfully:
"Great natural light in here.
The kitchen and bedroom are combined —"

Xiao Lin looked around.

"Combined, or is the kitchen also the bedroom also the living room?"

"We call it a 'multi-functional living space.'"

"How much?"

The agent named a number.

Xiao Lin went quiet.

"Can I just rent the bathroom?
I spend enough time in there.
Seems more practical."

Agent: "The bathroom isn't available separately,
but the loft upstairs might interest you."

"What does 'loft' mean here?"

"It means you hit the ceiling if you sit up too fast."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone currently apartment hunting — ideally right after they've toured their fifth 'multi-functional living space' in a row. They'll laugh, but it'll be the kind of laugh that's a little bit crying.
variations: Xiao Lin said later: "I've decided to just live at the office. I'm there more than I'm home anyway, and at least rent is free." Half joke, half actual plan.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「健康檢查結果一切正常，但你還是覺得有什麼不對」式幽默，搭配老化和存在焦慮，場景對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
老吳做完健康檢查，醫生說：
「一切指數都很正常，
你非常健康。」

老吳點點頭，回到家。

他坐在椅子上想了很久。

然後打電話給兒子：
「醫生說我很健康。」

「那很好啊，爸。」

「對，很好。
但我就是覺得……哪裡不對勁。」

「哪裡不對勁？」

「就是……如果身體沒問題，
那這種不對勁的感覺是從哪來的？」

電話沉默了一下。

兒子說：「爸，那個叫做……人生。」

老吳：「醫生有沒有辦法開藥？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給那個健康檢查一切正常、但還是每天覺得哪裡不對勁的人——存在焦慮是唯一一個連醫生都只能聳肩的症狀
variations: 老吳的兒子後來說：「我帶他去看了心理師。心理師說那種感覺很正常。老爸聽完說：那代表心理師也治不了，對嗎？心理師愣了一下說：……基本上對。」

en.content:
Old Wu finished his checkup. The doctor said:
"All your numbers are completely normal.
You're in excellent health."

Old Wu nodded and went home.

He sat in a chair for a long time.

Then he called his son:
"The doctor says I'm healthy."

"That's great, Dad."

"Right. Great.
But I still feel like... something's off."

"Off how?"

"Like... if there's nothing wrong with my body,
where is this feeling coming from?"

Silence on the phone.

Son said: "Dad. That's called... life."

Old Wu: "Can the doctor prescribe something for that?"
en.editorNote: Tell this to anyone who got a clean bill of health but still feels vaguely wrong every day — existential dread is the one symptom even doctors just shrug at.
variations: His son eventually took him to see a therapist. The therapist said the feeling was completely normal. Old Wu said: 'So that means you can't fix it either, right?' The therapist paused. '...Basically, yes.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「拖延型人格碰上遺囑」的荒謬前提，以待辦事項清單形式呈現，場景與文字完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿德的待辦事項清單最後幾行：

「整理房間」——拖了三年。
「回那封重要的信」——拖了七個月。
「和老朋友約吃飯」——拖了兩年半。
「寫遺囑」——拖了……

他往上算了一下。

拖了比以上所有加起來都還久的時間。

他想了一下，
在「寫遺囑」後面加了一個新項目：

「先寫遺囑草稿」

這樣感覺比較容易開始。

然後他把整張清單放回抽屜。

明天再說。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個把「寫遺囑」當成總有一天要做的事的人——最地獄的地方是，大家都知道這件事應該做，但它永遠排在「整理房間」的後面
variations: 阿德後來說：「我後來把清單上所有的事都加了『草稿』兩個字。整理房間草稿。遺囑草稿。回信草稿。這樣每一件事感覺都像是在進行了。」

en.content:
The last few lines of A-De's to-do list:

"Clean the apartment" — postponed for three years.
"Reply to that important email" — postponed for seven months.
"Schedule dinner with old friends" — postponed for two and a half years.
"Write a will" — postponed for...

He counted backward.

Longer than all the others combined.

He thought for a moment,
then added a new item below "Write a will":

"Draft outline for will"

That felt easier to start.

Then he put the whole list back in the drawer.

Tomorrow.
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who treats 'write a will' as one of those things they'll get to someday — the darkest part is that everyone knows they should, but it always ends up behind 'clean the apartment.'
variations: A-De said later: "I added the word 'draft' to everything on my list. Draft: clean apartment. Draft: will. Draft: reply to email. Suddenly every item felt like it was in progress."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「在葬禮上哭錯事情」的荒謬情感位移概念，以台灣家庭情境呈現，場景與對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿輝參加阿公的告別式，
哭得很傷心。

媽媽以為他在悼念阿公，
感動地拍了拍他的背。

典禮結束後，媽媽問：
「你和阿公感情很好嗎？
我不知道你這麼難過。」

阿輝擦了擦眼淚。

「阿公走得太突然……
我還沒跟他說我把他的老相機摔壞了。
現在……永遠沒有機會道歉了。」

媽媽沉默了一下。

「……你哭的是這個？」

阿輝認真地點頭。

「但我也有為阿公哭，
只是比相機哭少一點。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 那種在某個時刻突然湧上來的奇怪罪惡感，往往不是我們以為的那一種——阿輝是誠實的，只是誠實得有點讓人沉默
variations: 媽媽後來說：「阿公知道的話，可能會笑——因為他最討厭別人把他當成悲劇。但如果你真的那麼在意那台相機，他會說：去修嘛，修不好再買一台。」

en.content:
A-Hui cried hard at his grandfather's memorial service.

His mom assumed he was grieving,
and touched, patted him on the back.

Afterward, she asked:
"Were you two very close?
I didn't know you'd take it this hard."

A-Hui wiped his eyes.

"Grandpa left so suddenly...
I never got to tell him I broke his old camera.
Now... there's no chance to apologize."

Mom went quiet for a moment.

"...You're crying about that?"

A-Hui nodded sincerely.

"I cried for Grandpa too.
Just a little less than for the camera."
en.editorNote: The strange guilt that surfaces at unexpected moments is rarely the kind we expect — A-Hui is being honest, just honest enough to leave everyone a little speechless.
variations: Mom said later: "If Grandpa knew, he'd probably laugh — he hated being treated like a tragedy. And if you really felt that bad about the camera, he'd say: just get it fixed. Or buy another one."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自Joe Lennon關於「存在焦慮與日常生活」的暗黑漫畫，以氣象播報為人生隱喻，場景與對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
氣象播報員說：

「明天有百分之六十的機率下雨，
百分之四十晴天。」

阿翔在沙發上想了一下。

「人生其實就是一份天氣預報——
你永遠不知道是哪個百分之幾，
只能帶著傘出門，
然後看著傘坐在包包裡從來沒有用到，
因為今天居然沒下雨。」

朋友：「那如果真的下雨呢？」

阿翔：「那你就是忘記帶傘的那一天。」

「所以不管怎樣都是這樣？」

「對。
但氣象預報員還是每天都來上班。
這一點，我覺得挺勇敢的。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給那種覺得「做了準備也沒用，不做也沒用」的人聽——最後那句關於氣象員的話，是整個笑話裡最認真的一句，也是最地獄的一句
variations: 阿翔後來說：「我買了一把自動折疊傘，每天帶著。帶了三個月，沒用到一次。第四個月的第一天，忘了帶。你猜那天怎樣？」朋友說：「下大雨？」阿翔：「暴雨。完美的暴雨。」

en.content:
The weather forecaster said:

"Tomorrow there's a sixty percent chance of rain
and a forty percent chance of sun."

A-Xiang sat on the couch thinking.

"Life is basically a weather forecast —
you never know which percentage you're in.
You bring an umbrella just in case,
then watch it sit in your bag all day unused
because it didn't rain after all."

Friend: "And if it does rain?"

A-Xiang: "Then it's the one day you forgot the umbrella."

"So either way, it goes like that?"

"Yes.
But the forecaster still shows up for work every day.
I think that's actually kind of brave."
en.editorNote: Tell this to anyone who feels like 'being prepared doesn't help, but not preparing doesn't either' — the last line about the forecaster is the most sincere line in the whole joke, and also the darkest.
variations: A-Xiang said later: "I bought a compact auto-open umbrella and carried it every day for three months. Never used it once. First day of the fourth month, I forgot it. You know what happened?" Friend: "Heavy rain?" A-Xiang: "A downpour. A perfect, complete downpour."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「訃聞確認自己還活著」的梗，以台灣老一輩早晨習慣為情境，場景與對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿伯每天早上六點準時起床，
做的第一件事不是刷牙，不是泡茶。

是翻開報紙找訃聞版。

老婆說：「你每天看那個幹嘛？」

阿伯說：「確認一下我今天有沒有上去。」

老婆：「……」

「沒有就繼續過日子。
有的話就不用麻煩起床了。」

老婆沉默了一下。

「那你找到自己的時候要記得叫我。」

「好，不過那時候可能比較難叫你了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 說給那種習慣用黑色幽默面對老年的長輩——阿伯的邏輯其實完全沒有漏洞，只是這個檢查清單有點讓人不知道該笑還是該沉默
variations: 進階版：阿伯後來把這件事講給孫子聽。孫子問：「阿公，那如果哪天你真的看到自己的名字呢？」阿伯想了三秒：「那就表示我今天比較早知道消息。」

en.content:
Every morning at six sharp, Grandpa gets up.
The first thing he does — not brush his teeth, not brew tea.

He opens the newspaper to the obituaries.

Grandma asks: "Why do you read that every single day?"

Grandpa says: "Just checking if I'm in there."

Grandma: "..."

"If not, I carry on with the day.
If yes — well, no point getting out of bed."

Grandma paused.

"If you do find yourself in there, let me know."

"Sure. Though by then it might be hard to reach you."
en.editorNote: Tell this to the elder who uses dark humor to make peace with getting old — Grandpa's logic is airtight. It's just a checklist that leaves you unsure whether to laugh or go quiet.
variations: Extended: Grandpa told the story to his grandson. Grandson asked: "What if you actually see your name one day?" Grandpa thought for three seconds: "Then I'll know the news a little earlier than everyone else."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「偷了日記的人死了」的英文梗，改寫為台灣通訊錄情境，完全重新創作人物與對話

zh-tw.content:
阿偉的手機壞掉，通訊錄全部不見了。

他媽媽說：「你之前那些朋友都還在嗎？」

阿偉想了一下：「不知道。
我本來有一千三百個聯絡人。
現在一個都找不到，等於全部重新開始。」

媽媽：「那不是很慘？」

阿偉：「還好吧。
真正需要聯絡的人，自然會再聯絡我。

其他那一千兩百九十幾個——
就當成我舉辦了一場沉默的告別式，
把他們都好好地送走了。」

媽媽：「那你有難過嗎？」

阿偉停了一下。

「我難過的是……
手機壞掉之後，
主動聯絡我的只有三個人。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 一個手機壞掉引發的人際盤點，最地獄的地方是最後那個數字——三個。傳給那個正在懷疑自己到底有幾個真朋友的人
variations: 後續版：阿偉說：「後來我決定不備份通訊錄了。每隔幾年讓它自然清空一次，等於是強迫自己重新確認一遍哪些人還在。這樣其實比較誠實。」

en.content:
A-Wei's phone died and wiped out his entire contacts list.

His mom asked: "Are all those friends of yours still around?"

A-Wei thought about it: "No idea.
I had thirteen hundred contacts.
Now they're all gone — like starting from zero."

Mom: "Isn't that terrible?"

A-Wei: "Not really.
The people who matter will reach out again on their own.

The other twelve-hundred-something —
let's say I quietly held a farewell ceremony
and sent them all off properly."

Mom: "Did it make you sad?"

A-Wei paused.

"What made me sad was...
after the phone died,
only three people reached out first."
en.editorNote: A phone crash that turns into a relationship audit. The darkest part is the final number — three. Send to anyone quietly wondering how many real friends they actually have.
variations: Follow-up: A-Wei said: "I decided not to back up my contacts anymore. Let it wipe clean every few years — it's like a forced reset to confirm who's still there. Honestly, more accurate that way."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「努力工作比退休更致命」的英文梗，改寫為台灣職場情境，人物與對話完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿仁在同一家公司做了三十八年，
終於等到退休的那一天。

同事們幫他辦了告別茶會，
送了一個刻名字的紀念牌。

阿仁拿著牌子，笑著說：
「你們知道嗎？
這是我這輩子第一次，
公司花錢在我身上，
是為了讓我離開，不是讓我留下來。」

大家都笑了。

阿仁低頭看了看牌子，
補了一句：

「上面只有名字和年份。
沒有我做過什麼。
感覺和墓碑差不多。」

笑聲停了幾秒。
zh-tw.editorNote: 退休派對上的那一句，說出了所有人心裡隱隱有但不敢講的事——三十八年濃縮成名字加年份。傳給即將退休或剛退休的人，或那個正在懷疑工作意義的人
variations: 阿仁後來說：「不過墓碑是讓人記得你的。紀念牌是讓你記得自己離開了。這兩件事好像不太一樣，但我還沒想清楚哪個比較好。」

en.content:
A-Ren worked at the same company for thirty-eight years.
The day he finally retired arrived.

Colleagues threw him a farewell tea party
and presented a commemorative plaque engraved with his name.

A-Ren held it up, smiling:
"You know what? This is the first time in my life
the company spent money on me
to make me leave — not to keep me here."

Everyone laughed.

A-Ren looked down at the plaque
and added quietly:

"Just my name and the years.
Nothing about what I actually did.
Feel a bit like a gravestone."

The laughter stopped for a few seconds.
en.editorNote: That one line at the retirement party says what everyone quietly senses but doesn't dare voice — thirty-eight years condensed into a name and two dates. Send to someone retiring, or anyone quietly questioning what their work actually means.
variations: A-Ren said afterward: "Though a gravestone is meant for others to remember you. A plaque is for you to remember you left. Those aren't quite the same thing. I haven't figured out which is better yet."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「死亡與喜劇表演」的英文梗，改寫為台灣婚禮演講情境，人物與場景完全原創

zh-tw.content:
婚禮上，新郎的老朋友阿建被叫上台致詞。

他清了清喉嚨，說：

「我認識志明二十三年了。
這二十三年裡，他做過很多讓我擔心的決定。」

賓客笑了。

「但我今天站在這裡，
不是要說他做過哪些壞決定——
是要說，這一次，
他終於做了一個讓我放心的。」

大家鼓掌。

阿建停頓了一秒，補充：

「雖然放心的理由是：
從今以後，如果他又做了什麼壞決定，
至少旁邊有人可以攔他了。」

掌聲更大了，
新娘笑了，
志明往椅子裡縮了一點。
zh-tw.editorNote: 婚禮致詞最好用的格式：先讚美，再插刀，最後讓新娘笑——這個笑話的結構本身就是一個教學，適合傳給被叫去致詞但不知道講什麼的朋友
variations: 阿建後來說：「我本來還有第二段。說從今以後他的壞決定至少需要兩個人同意。但我看新娘的表情，覺得她已經懂了，就跳過了。」

en.content:
At the wedding, the groom's old friend A-Jian was called up to give a toast.

He cleared his throat and said:

"I've known Zhi-Ming for twenty-three years.
In those twenty-three years, he's made a lot of decisions that worried me."

The guests laughed.

"But I'm not up here today
to talk about his bad calls —
I'm here to say that this time,
he finally made one that lets me breathe."

Applause.

A-Jian paused, then added:

"Although the reason I can breathe easy is:
from now on, whenever he makes another questionable decision,
there's at least someone standing next to him who can stop him."

Louder applause.
The bride laughed.
Zhi-Ming sank a little lower in his chair.
en.editorNote: The ideal wedding toast structure: compliment, then twist the knife, then make the bride laugh — the joke's architecture is a tutorial in itself. Pass to any friend who got roped into giving a speech and has no idea what to say.
variations: A-Jian said later: "I had a second part planned — that from now on his bad decisions would require a two-person sign-off. But I looked at the bride's face and could tell she already understood. So I skipped it."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「人生保險與年齡」的英文梗，改寫為台灣健檢情境，角色與對話完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿德四十五歲那年，終於去做了人生第一次完整健康檢查。

報告出來，醫生翻了翻，說：
「整體來說，你的身體狀況——
跟你的車差不多。」

阿德問：「什麼意思？」

醫生說：「就是說，零件都還在，
引擎也還能發動，
只是每個地方都開始有點異音，
而且你也不太記得上次保養是什麼時候了。」

阿德沉默了一下。

「那……還能開多久？」

醫生把報告蓋上：
「要看你怎麼用。
但有一件事可以確定——
這種車，已經過了可以隨便操的年紀了。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給剛過四十、還沒去做健檢的朋友——這個笑話比任何說教都有效，因為它完全沒有在罵你，只是說了實話
variations: 阿德後來說：「我去問了保養費用。醫生說，這個年紀每年保養比新車貴，但換一台也沒有比較划算。我覺得他不是在說車。」

en.content:
At forty-five, A-De finally went for his first full health checkup.

When the results came back, the doctor flipped through them and said:
"Overall, your health is — about the same as your car."

A-De asked: "What does that mean?"

Doctor: "It means all the parts are still there,
the engine still starts,
but there are a few odd noises coming from various places,
and you can't quite remember the last time you got it serviced."

A-De was quiet for a moment.

"So… how much longer can I drive it?"

The doctor set down the report:
"Depends on how you treat it.
But one thing's for sure —
this model is past the age where you can just floor it and hope for the best."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who just hit forty and keeps dodging the doctor — this lands better than any lecture because it never scolds, it just tells the truth with a straight face.
variations: A-De said afterward: "I asked about maintenance costs. The doctor said at this age, annual upkeep runs higher than a new car, but replacing it isn't really cost-effective either. I don't think he was talking about the car."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「AI 比治療師更了解你」的英文梗，改寫為台灣推薦演算法情境，對話與角色完全原創

zh-tw.content:
小薇用串流平台用了三年。

有一天，演算法推了一部新影集給她。
簡介寫著：「為你推薦：關於一個人習慣在深夜獨自喝茶、反覆看同一部老片、偶爾哭一下但隔天照常上班的故事。」

小薇愣了五秒。

然後按了播放。

看完第一集，她覺得有點不舒服，
不是因為難看，
是因為看起來太像在看自己的直播。

她打開評論區，
第一則留言寫著：
「這部根本是紀錄片。」

她按了讚。

然後把平板蓋起來，
去泡了一杯茶。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個有點孤獨但不想說出口的朋友——不要加任何說明，讓她自己看完，看她有沒有回你說「這根本在說我」
variations: 小薇後來把帳號的觀看紀錄清掉了。但第二天演算法還是推了同樣的東西。它說：「根據你的清除行為，你可能喜歡這個。」她又按了播放。

en.content:
Xiao-Wei had been using the streaming platform for three years.

One day, the algorithm pushed her a new series.
The description read: "Recommended for you: A story about someone who makes tea alone late at night, rewatches the same old shows on loop, cries occasionally, and still shows up to work the next morning."

Xiao-Wei stared at it for five seconds.

Then she hit play.

By the end of episode one, she felt a little unsettled —
not because it was bad,
but because it felt too much like watching her own livestream.

She opened the comments.
The top review said:
"This is basically a documentary."

She liked it.

Then she closed the tablet
and went to make herself some tea.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who's a little lonely but won't say it out loud — no explanation needed. Just send it and wait to see if they reply "this is literally me."
variations: Xiao-Wei later cleared her watch history. The next day, the algorithm recommended the same thing again. It said: "Based on your deletion behavior, you might enjoy this." She hit play again.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「遺囑與家人反應」的英文梗，改寫為台灣家族會議情境，人物與情節完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿公八十歲那年，把全家人叫來開了一個「重要的家庭會議」。

大家都以為他要交代後事。

阿公清了清喉嚨，說：
「我這輩子存了一些錢，
想在死之前決定怎麼分。」

全家人屏氣凝神。

「思來想去，
我決定都捐出去。」

沉默。

然後阿公加了一句：

「不過放心，
我捐的是你們這些年孝順我的那份心意——
就是那些問候電話、過年紅包、假日探望……
我會代你們捐給慈善機構，
折算成功德，
讓你們死後比較好過。」

沉默更久了。

最後是大兒子先笑出來，
然後全家都笑了，
除了二兒子。

他已經在心裡盤算這幾年電話打了幾通了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 過年家族聚會的時候說，或者傳給那個老是覺得自己孝順卻算不清帳的親戚——這個笑話讓大家都能笑，但每個人笑的點不一樣
variations: 阿公後來說：「不捐也可以，但你們要自己算，這些年加起來值多少。算出來之後再告訴我，我考慮一下要不要等值退還。」二兒子當場拿出手機開始算。

en.content:
When Grandpa turned eighty, he called the whole family together for an "important family meeting."

Everyone assumed he was about to settle his affairs.

Grandpa cleared his throat:
"I've saved a little money over the years
and I want to decide what to do with it before I die."

The room held its breath.

"After thinking it over,
I've decided to donate it all."

Silence.

Then Grandpa added:

"But don't worry.
What I'm donating is the filial love you've all shown me over the years —
the check-in calls, the Lunar New Year gifts, the weekend visits...
I'll donate it to charity on your behalf,
converted into merit points,
so things go a little smoother for you when your time comes."

Longer silence.

Finally the eldest son laughed.
Then the whole family laughed.
Except the second son.

He was already counting in his head how many calls he'd actually made.
en.editorNote: Say it at a Lunar New Year family gathering, or send to the relative who always claims to be the most devoted — everyone laughs, but for completely different reasons.
variations: Grandpa added: "You could also opt out of the donation — but then you'd have to calculate what those years were actually worth, and tell me. I'll consider reimbursing you at face value." The second son immediately pulled out his phone and started adding things up.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「失眠與存在危機」的英文梗，改寫為台灣深夜思考情境，完全原創敘事

zh-tw.content:
阿明數了十七隻羊，還是睡不著。

他換個姿勢，盯著天花板，
開始思考一個問題：

「我這一生，到底完成了什麼？」

他列了一下清單：
高中畢業、大學畢業、找到工作、
換過三份工作、交過兩任女朋友、
養過一隻貓（後來跑掉了）。

他看著這份清單，
覺得用來填維基百科的個人條目大概不太夠。

但轉念想想——
維基百科上有條目的人，
也不見得都睡得著。

他閉上眼睛，
繼續數羊。

第十八隻羊也跑掉了。
zh-tw.editorNote: 凌晨兩點還在滑手機的時候傳給自己，或傳給那個也睡不著的朋友——失眠的人不需要建議，只需要知道有人也在數羊
variations: 第二天阿明去上班，主管問他：「昨晚睡得好嗎？」他說：「睡得還行——就是做了個夢，夢到自己在維基百科上有條目，但內容只有三行。」主管說：「那很正常，我的話連三行都不到。」

en.content:
A-Ming counted seventeen sheep and still couldn't sleep.

He shifted position, stared at the ceiling,
and started thinking about a question:

"What exactly have I accomplished in this life?"

He made a list:
graduated high school, graduated college, got a job,
changed jobs three times, dated two people,
had a cat that eventually ran away.

He looked at the list
and figured it probably wasn't enough to fill a Wikipedia entry.

But then again —
people with Wikipedia entries
don't necessarily sleep any better.

He closed his eyes
and kept counting.

The eighteenth sheep ran away too.
en.editorNote: Send to yourself at 2am while scrolling, or to the friend who's also awake for no good reason — people who can't sleep don't need advice, they just need to know someone else is counting sheep too.
variations: The next day A-Ming's manager asked: "Sleep well?" He said: "Not bad — just dreamed I had a Wikipedia page, but the whole entry was only three lines." Manager: "That's normal. Mine wouldn't even hit three."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「搬家與人生重置」的英文梗，改寫為台灣租屋搬家情境，完全原創對話與結局

zh-tw.content:
阿珍搬了第七次家。

每次搬家，她都告訴自己：
「這次是新開始。」

第一次，她扔掉了一個前男友留下的馬克杯。
第三次，她扔掉了大學時買的「夢想計畫」筆記本。
第五次，她扔掉了一整箱「等我有空再看」的書。

這次，她看著裝箱清單，
發現沒什麼可扔的了。

然後她意識到——
這七次「新開始」，
她搬走了所有東西，
唯獨沒辦法打包的，
是她自己。

她把最後一個箱子封起來，
貼上標籤。

上面寫：「雜物」。

然後她想了一下，
把標籤撕掉，
改成：「重要的事」。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在搬家、或正在考慮人生重來的朋友——結尾那個改標籤的動作，你想解讀成自嘲或是自我肯定都可以，那是讀者的選擇
variations: 阿珍搬進新家的第一個晚上，坐在空蕩蕩的客廳地板上，打給媽媽。媽媽問：「新家好嗎？」她說：「還行，感覺很新。」媽媽說：「新就好，住久了就有家的感覺了。」她說：「嗯。」但她想的是，上一個地方她也住了兩年，最後還是打包走了。

en.content:
A-Zhen moved for the seventh time.

Every time she moved, she told herself:
"This is a fresh start."

First move: she threw out a mug an ex had left behind.
Third move: she threw out the "life plan" notebook she'd bought in college.
Fifth move: she threw out a whole box of "I'll read these when I have time" books.

This time, she looked at the packing list
and realized there wasn't much left to throw away.

Then it hit her —
through all seven "fresh starts,"
she'd moved away from everything.
The one thing she couldn't put in a box
was herself.

She sealed the last box
and reached for a label.

She wrote: "Misc."

Then she thought about it,
peeled it off,
and wrote instead: "Things That Matter."
en.editorNote: Send to a friend who's moving, or anyone quietly considering starting over — that final label swap can be read as self-deprecation or self-affirmation. That part's up to the reader.
variations: On A-Zhen's first night in the new place, she sat on the bare living room floor and called her mom. Mom asked: "How's the new place?" She said: "Fine. Feels very new." Mom: "New is good — it'll feel like home once you've lived there a while." She said: "Yeah." But what she was thinking was: she'd lived in the last place for two years, and still packed up and left.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「銀行帳戶與存在焦慮」的英文梗，改寫為台灣 ATM 情境，原創對話

zh-tw.content:
小婷站在 ATM 前面，刷卡，輸入密碼。

螢幕跳出訊息：
「您今日的提款已超過餘額限制。」

她站著沒動。

後面排隊的人客氣地說：
「小姐，可以麻煩您讓一下嗎？」

小婷回頭，平靜地說：
「不好意思，我只是在等它告訴我，
我這輩子的提款，
是不是也已經超過餘額限制了。」

後面的人安靜地讓出兩個身位。

沒人想接這個話。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給月底領薪水前一天的朋友——他會苦笑，因為這個焦慮太具體了，具體到他剛剛才在 ATM 前面想過一模一樣的事
variations: 進階版：ATM 螢幕又跳出一行——「請選擇是否列印明細表？」小婷點了「否」。她說：「有些事情，知道就好，不需要拿在手上。」

en.content:
Xiao-Ting stood at the ATM, swiped her card, entered her PIN.

The screen flashed:
"Today's withdrawal exceeds your available balance."

She didn't move.

The person behind her said politely:
"Miss, could you step aside?"

Xiao-Ting turned around and said calmly:
"Sorry — I'm just waiting for it to tell me
whether my lifetime withdrawals
have also exceeded my available balance."

The people behind her quietly stepped back a couple of paces.

Nobody wanted to touch that one.
en.editorNote: Send to a friend the day before payday — they'll laugh painfully, because the anxiety is too specific. They were standing at an ATM thinking the exact same thing this morning.
variations: Follow-up: the screen asked, "Print receipt?" Xiao-Ting tapped No. "Some things," she said, "are better just knowing. You don't need to hold them in your hand."

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「鬧鐘 vs. 人生意義」的英文梗，改寫為台灣上班族晨間情境，原創

zh-tw.content:
阿凱每天早上六點半被鬧鐘叫醒。

他按下貪睡，五分鐘後再響。
按下貪睡，五分鐘後再響。
按下貪睡——

第七次的時候，他終於坐起來，
看著鬧鐘，認真地問它：
「你每天這樣叫我，
你不會覺得，
我們倆其實都很可憐嗎？」

鬧鐘沒回答。
它只是繼續響。

因為這就是它的工作。
它甚至沒得貪睡。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每天早上要被鬧鐘虐七次的同事——尤其是那種把鬧鐘設成「6:30、6:35、6:40、6:45、6:50、6:55、7:00」的強迫症晨間受害者
variations: 後來阿凱換了一個會說「該起床了」的智慧鬧鐘。第一天它說：「該起床了。」阿凱回：「我知道。」第二天它說：「該起床了。」阿凱回：「我知道。」第七天它說：「該起床了。」阿凱回：「我知道，但是『該』跟『想』不是同一件事。」

en.content:
Kai gets woken up by his alarm at 6:30 every morning.

He hits snooze. Five minutes later, it goes off again.
He hits snooze. Five minutes later, it goes off again.
Snooze. Again. Snooze. Again.

On the seventh round, he finally sits up,
looks at the alarm clock, and asks it seriously:
"You wake me up like this every day.
Don't you ever feel like
we're both kind of pathetic?"

The alarm doesn't answer.
It just keeps ringing.

Because that's its job.
It doesn't even get to snooze.
en.editorNote: Send to the coworker who sets seven alarms at five-minute intervals every morning — they'll feel personally attacked, then thank you
variations: Kai eventually upgraded to a smart alarm that says "Time to get up." Day one, it said: "Time to get up." Kai replied: "I know." Day two, same thing. "I know." By day seven: "I know. But 'time to' and 'want to' are different things."

items.sourceNote: 原創——關於健身房年費與自我欺騙

zh-tw.content:
阿傑在一月辦了健身房年費。

一月，他去了五次。
二月，他去了兩次。
三月，他去了一次。
四月，他想起這件事的時候，已經是繳卡費的時候。

他打開帳單，看到健身房的扣款，
沉思了一下。

然後他做了一個決定：
他打電話去健身房。

「您好，請問是要約教練嗎？」
阿傑：「不是，我想問——
你們的年費，
可以算成『心安費』嗎？
因為我每個月看到扣款，
都會覺得自己『有在運動』。」

櫃台沉默了一下：
「先生，這是您的選擇。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給每年一月都會「重新開始」的朋友——他們會笑，但笑得很心虛，因為健身房年費的真正功能就是：讓你以為你已經在運動了
variations: 阿傑最後的結論是：健身房年費其實是現代人的贖罪券。你不去，但你付了錢，所以你的良心就可以暫時不用面對「我沒在運動」這件事——直到下一張帳單來，再贖一次。

en.content:
Jay signed up for a year at the gym in January.

January: he went five times.
February: twice.
March: once.
April: he remembered the gym existed when his credit card statement arrived.

He opened the bill, saw the gym charge,
thought about it for a while.

Then he made a decision.
He called the gym.

"Hi, would you like to book a trainer?"
Jay: "No, I have a question —
can I reclassify the annual fee
as a 'peace of mind' fee?
Because every month I see the charge,
and I feel like I'm 'working out.'"

The receptionist paused:
"Sir, that's your choice."
en.editorNote: Send to a friend who 'starts fresh' every January — they'll laugh, but guiltily, because the actual function of a gym membership is to convince you you're working out without having to do it
variations: Jay's final theory: a gym membership is a modern indulgence. You don't go, but you pay, and so your conscience gets a temporary pass on 'I'm not exercising' — until the next bill arrives, and you have to buy your way out again.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「LinkedIn 履歷與謊言」的英文梗，改寫為台灣求職情境，原創

zh-tw.content:
小芸更新了履歷。

她寫：「擅長跨部門溝通。」
（其實是被迫每天傳訊息給三個不同部門催進度。）

她寫：「具備抗壓性。」
（其實是哭完還會準時交報告。）

她寫：「樂於學習新事物。」
（其實是因為老闆不停換系統，她沒得選。）

她寫：「具備領導潛力。」
（其實是同事都離職了，沒人可以帶她。）

她按下儲存，看著螢幕說：
「我寫的每一句都是真的。
只是真的——
比看起來更慘。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給正在更新履歷的朋友——他會苦笑，因為履歷上每一條看起來光鮮的描述，背後都是某個崩潰的下午被磨出來的
variations: 進階版：小芸把履歷給男友看，男友說：「你寫得太低調了，加幾個關鍵字。」小芸：「比如？」男友：「比如『願景驅動』、『顛覆思維』、『跨領域整合』。」小芸：「這些是什麼意思？」男友：「沒人知道，但 HR 喜歡看到。」

en.content:
Yun updated her résumé.

She wrote: "Skilled at cross-functional communication."
(Translation: forced to chase three different teams every day.)

She wrote: "Strong under pressure."
(Translation: cries, then files the report on time anyway.)

She wrote: "Eager to learn new things."
(Translation: her boss keeps switching systems and she has no choice.)

She wrote: "Demonstrates leadership potential."
(Translation: everyone else quit, so there's no one left to mentor her.)

She hit save, stared at the screen, and said:
"Every line is technically true.
It's just that the truth
is sadder than it looks."
en.editorNote: Send to a friend updating their résumé — they'll laugh painfully, because every shiny bullet point on a CV was earned during some specific afternoon breakdown
variations: Yun showed the résumé to her boyfriend. He said: "It's too modest. Add some buzzwords." Yun: "Like what?" Him: "Vision-driven, disruptive thinker, cross-domain synergy." Yun: "What do those mean?" Him: "Nobody knows. But HR loves seeing them."

items.sourceNote: 原創——關於外送 app 與孤獨

zh-tw.content:
小柏一個人住，叫了外送。

外送員按門鈴，他開門，接過餐。
外送員說：「謝謝，慢用。」

小柏關上門的那一刻，突然意識到——

這是他今天第一次跟人說話。
而那句「謝謝，慢用」，
是他今天聽到過最溫柔的一句。

他坐下來吃飯，
打開外送 app，給了五星評價。

備註欄裡他寫：
「謝謝你今天跟我說話。」

外送員後來收到通知，
看了一眼，沒回應。

因為他自己回家以後，
也沒人會跟他說話。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給一個人住、最近覺得有點安靜的朋友——但小心，這個梗會打中很多人，包括傳的人自己
variations: 後來小柏發現，他開始期待外送來的那一刻——不是因為餓，而是因為那是他一天裡唯一會聽到「謝謝」的時候。他想了想，覺得這個發現比餓還可怕。

en.content:
Bo lives alone. He ordered delivery.

The driver rang the bell. Bo opened the door, took the food.
The driver said: "Thanks, enjoy your meal."

The moment Bo closed the door, it hit him —

that was the first time he'd spoken to a person all day.
And "thanks, enjoy your meal"
was the gentlest thing he'd heard today.

He sat down to eat,
opened the app, and left a five-star review.

In the comment box, he wrote:
"Thanks for talking to me today."

The driver got the notification later,
glanced at it, didn't reply.

Because when he got home,
there was no one waiting to talk to him either.
en.editorNote: Send to a friend who lives alone and has been quiet lately — but be careful, this one lands hard, including on the person sending it
variations: Bo eventually noticed he'd started looking forward to the delivery moment — not because he was hungry, but because it was the only time in his day he'd hear someone say "thanks." He thought about that for a while, and decided the realization was scarier than the hunger.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自2026年AI訂閱疊加的諷刺討論，改寫為台灣上班族訂閱現況，對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿凱打開銀行 app，看著每月扣款。

ChatGPT Plus、Claude Pro、Notion AI、
Midjourney、Cursor、Perplexity Pro——
加起來一個月一百多塊美金。

他坐在電腦前，問 ChatGPT：
「我該不該取消其中幾個訂閱？」

ChatGPT 給了三千字的分析。

他複製貼到 Claude，問：
「這個建議客觀嗎？」

Claude 又給了三千字。

他打開 Perplexity，搜：
「AI 訂閱會不會讓人變笨？」

看完之後他關掉電腦，
沉思了很久。

然後他打開 ChatGPT，問：
「我剛剛在思考什麼？」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個每個月看到信用卡帳單才想起自己訂了什麼的朋友——AI 訂閱的可怕之處是，你連「該不該取消」這個問題都要問 AI
variations: 阿凱最後的結論是：他訂的不是 AI，是「有人陪他思考」的錯覺。真正的問題是，那些 AI 加起來，比他自己還更了解他在猶豫什麼。

en.content:
Kai opened his banking app and stared at the monthly charges.

ChatGPT Plus, Claude Pro, Notion AI,
Midjourney, Cursor, Perplexity Pro —
over a hundred dollars a month, total.

He sat at his desk and asked ChatGPT:
"Should I cancel some of these subscriptions?"

ChatGPT gave him three thousand words of analysis.

He pasted it into Claude and asked:
"Is this advice objective?"

Claude gave him another three thousand words.

He opened Perplexity and searched:
"Do AI subscriptions make people dumber?"

After reading the results, he closed his laptop
and sat there for a while.

Then he opened ChatGPT again:
"What was I just thinking about?"
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who only remembers what they're subscribed to when the credit card bill arrives — the scary thing about AI subscriptions is that you have to ask AI whether you should cancel them
variations: Kai's final theory: he wasn't paying for AI, he was paying for the illusion of someone thinking alongside him. The real problem was that, combined, those AIs understood his hesitation better than he did.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自食品保存期限的存在主義反差幽默，場景與對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
小婷在超市拿起一盒優格，
看了一眼保存期限。

還剩兩天。

她猶豫地放回去，拿了另一盒——
保存期限：兩個月。

結帳的時候，她突然想到：

「我自己的保存期限是什麼時候？
沒有印在我身上。
沒有人知道。」

她回家把那盒優格放進冰箱，
忘了它的存在。

兩個月後，她打開冰箱，
看到那盒已經過期一週的優格。

她笑了一下。

「我比你撐得久。
但你比我有寫期限。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個看到食物過期會猶豫很久才丟的朋友——保存期限的真正意義不是食物，是讓我們意識到自己其實也有，只是沒寫出來
variations: 小婷後來把那盒優格丟掉之前，又看了一眼日期。她想：「至少它在過期之前，是被冰箱記得的。」這句話她沒說出口，但記了很久。

en.content:
Ting picked up a yogurt at the supermarket
and glanced at the expiration date.

Two days left.

She hesitated, put it back, grabbed another one —
best before: two months from now.

At checkout, a thought hit her:

"What's my expiration date?
It's not printed on me.
No one knows."

She went home, put the yogurt in the fridge,
and forgot it existed.

Two months later, she opened the fridge
and saw it — one week expired.

She smiled a little.

"I outlasted you.
But at least yours was written down."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who stares at expired food for ten minutes before throwing it out — the real point of expiration dates isn't the food, it's the reminder that we have one too, just unprinted
variations: Before throwing the yogurt out, Ting glanced at the date one more time. She thought: "At least the fridge remembered it before it expired." She didn't say it out loud. She remembered the thought for a long time.

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自繁瑣官僚流程的黑色幽默，改寫為台灣戶政事務所場景，對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿德去戶政事務所辦一份證明。

櫃台說：「您要先抽號碼牌。」
他抽了。等了四十分鐘。

櫃台說：「您填的這張表錯了，要去隔壁拿正確的版本。」
他去了，拿了，回來。

櫃台說：「這份要本人簽名。」
他簽了。

櫃台說：「但簽錯位置。要重抽號碼牌。」

阿德深呼吸，重抽。又等四十分鐘。

輪到他時，櫃台換了一個人。
新櫃台看了一下：
「您這個案件，原承辦今天請假。
建議您改天再來。」

阿德沉默地走出大門。

外面陽光很好。
他突然覺得——

「也許這份證明，根本不是我需要的。
是這個系統需要我繼續來證明它存在。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給任何一個在政府機關被退件三次以上的人——他會苦笑著點頭，因為阿德最後那句話，是所有公部門大廳裡最安靜也最真實的領悟
variations: 阿德最後沒有再去。三個月後他發現，他其實不需要那份證明。但這件事他沒告訴任何人，因為他怕說出來，會被要求填一份「不需要證明的證明」。

en.content:
De went to the government office to get a certificate.

The clerk said: "Take a number first."
He did. Waited forty minutes.

The clerk said: "This form is wrong. Get the correct version next door."
He got it. Came back.

The clerk said: "This needs your signature."
He signed.

The clerk said: "Wrong spot. Take a new number."

De inhaled, took a new number. Waited another forty minutes.

When his turn came, a different clerk was at the desk.
The new clerk looked at the file:
"The original case officer is out today.
Please come back another time."

De walked out in silence.

The sunlight outside was nice.
A thought hit him:

"Maybe this certificate isn't what I needed.
Maybe the system needed me to keep coming back
to prove that it still exists."
en.editorNote: Send to anyone who's been bounced three or more times at a government office — they'll smile painfully and nod, because De's last line is the quietest, truest thought in every bureaucracy lobby
variations: De never went back. Three months later he realized he didn't actually need the certificate. He never told anyone — he was afraid that admitting it out loud would somehow require him to fill out a 'certificate of not needing a certificate.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自現代職場 AI 取代焦慮的諷刺漫畫，劇情完全原創改寫

zh-tw.content:
公司導入 AI 之後，小華被叫進老闆辦公室。

老闆說：「我們不是要裁你。
是要你訓練 AI 取代你。」

小華問：「訓練完之後呢？」

老闆笑了笑：「你會升職。
升職成 AI 的『人類監督員』。
薪水不變，工時減半。」

小華大喜：「真的嗎？」

「對，工時減半——
因為 AI 做事比你快兩倍，
你只需要在它出錯的時候按確認。」

三個月後，小華發現 AI 從來不出錯。

他坐在辦公桌前，
盯著一個永遠不會亮的紅色按鈕，
領著薪水。

那一刻他終於明白——
原來最殘忍的取代，
不是讓你消失，
是讓你留下來，看著自己變得多餘。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個最近被公司要求「擁抱 AI」的朋友——他會先笑，然後盯著螢幕發呆很久，因為這個笑話講的其實不是 AI，是「被需要」這件事本身正在過期
variations: 進階版：小華後來真的等到 AI 出錯了一次。他興奮地按下確認鍵——結果系統跳出訊息：「感謝您的確認，本次錯誤為系統故意安排，用於評估監督員的反應時間。您的反應時間：48 秒，未達標準。」

en.content:
After the company rolled out AI, Hua was called into the boss's office.

The boss said: "We're not laying you off.
We just need you to train the AI to replace you."

Hua asked: "And after that?"

The boss smiled: "You'll be promoted.
To 'Human Supervisor' of the AI.
Same salary, half the hours."

Hua lit up: "Really?"

"Yes, half the hours —
because the AI works twice as fast as you.
You just press confirm when it makes a mistake."

Three months later, Hua realized the AI never made mistakes.

He sat at his desk,
staring at a red button that would never light up,
collecting his paycheck.

That's when it hit him —
the cruelest replacement
isn't being erased.
It's being kept around to watch yourself become unnecessary.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend whose company recently told them to 'embrace AI' — they'll laugh, then stare at their monitor for a long time. Because this joke isn't really about AI. It's about how 'being needed' is the thing that's quietly expiring.
variations: Follow-up: Hua finally caught the AI making a mistake. He excitedly pressed confirm — and the screen popped up: 'Thank you for confirming. This error was intentionally injected to evaluate supervisor reaction time. Your time: 48 seconds. Below standard.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自虛無主義迷因關於日常生活儀式感的諷刺，原創改寫為手機通知場景

zh-tw.content:
阿凱今天手機響了 47 次。

45 次是廣告通知。
1 次是健身 App 提醒他今天還沒運動。
1 次是外送 App 推薦「您可能想念的餐廳」。

沒有一則是人類。

他坐在床上，
看著自己的手機螢幕。

突然有點感動——
至少這個世界，
還有 47 個系統記得他存在。

他打開外送 App，
點了那家「您可能想念的餐廳」。

餐點送達的時候，
外送員放下袋子就走了。

阿凱關上門，
手機又響了一聲。

是外送 App：「請為您的訂單評分。」

他笑了一下，給了五顆星。
備註欄寫：「謝謝你今天問我感覺怎麼樣。」
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個說自己「不需要朋友」的朋友——他會盯著螢幕看很久，因為阿凱備註欄那句話，是所有獨居者半夜按下傳送鍵時最不敢承認的願望
variations: 外送 App 後來回覆阿凱：「感謝您的回饋。本次評分將用於改善我們的服務品質。」阿凱看著這句話想：「至少還有人，會說『感謝您的回饋』。」

en.content:
Kai's phone buzzed 47 times today.

45 were ads.
1 was a fitness app reminding him he hadn't worked out.
1 was a food delivery app recommending "a restaurant you might miss."

Not a single one was a human.

He sat on his bed,
staring at his screen.

A strange warmth hit him —
at least 47 systems out there
still remembered he existed.

He opened the delivery app
and ordered from that "restaurant you might miss."

When the food arrived,
the driver dropped the bag and left.

Kai closed the door.
His phone buzzed again.

The delivery app: "Please rate your order."

He smiled a little. Gave it five stars.
In the comments he wrote:
"Thanks for asking how I'm doing today."
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who insists they 'don't need friends' — they'll stare at it for a while. Kai's comment is the thing every person living alone secretly wants to type at 2am, and never quite dares to.
variations: The app later replied: 'Thank you for your feedback. Your rating will help us improve our service.' Kai read it and thought: 'At least somebody still says thank you.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自關於睡眠與生產力的諷刺笑話，改寫為台灣加班文化場景，對白完全原創

zh-tw.content:
醫生對小婷說：「妳需要多睡一點。
每天至少八小時。」

小婷苦笑：「醫生，那我什麼時候工作？」

醫生：「下班後就不要工作。」

小婷：「但下班後我才有時間運動、做飯、跟家人講話、整理人生。」

醫生想了想：「那妳什麼時候有自己的時間？」

小婷：「睡覺的時候。
但如果我睡八小時，
就沒有自己的時間了。」

醫生沉默了很久。

最後說：「我建議妳，
繼續失眠。
至少那是妳唯一不用交給別人的時間。」

小婷愣了一下，
然後突然懂了——
原來她這幾年熬夜，
不是因為睡不著。
是因為，那是她一天裡，
唯一還屬於自己的東西。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個總說「等忙完這陣子就好好睡」的朋友——他會在凌晨兩點看到這則訊息，然後停下手邊的事，盯著天花板很久，因為小婷那句話他講不出口，但他每天都在這樣活著
variations: 後來小婷真的開始早睡。一個月後她去回診，醫生問她感覺如何。她說：「睡得很好，但我覺得我消失了。」醫生點點頭：「恭喜，這就是健康的代價。」

en.content:
The doctor told Ting: "You need more sleep.
At least eight hours a night."

Ting smiled bitterly: "Then when do I work, doctor?"

Doctor: "Don't work after hours."

Ting: "But after hours is when I exercise, cook, talk to my family, sort out my life."

Doctor thought about it: "So when do you get time for yourself?"

Ting: "When I sleep.
But if I sleep eight hours,
I have no time for myself."

The doctor was quiet for a long time.

Finally: "My professional advice —
keep your insomnia.
It's the only time you don't owe anyone."

Ting froze.

And suddenly she understood —
she hadn't been staying up
because she couldn't sleep.

She'd been staying up
because those hours
were the only thing in her day
that still belonged to her.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying 'I'll sleep properly once this busy patch is over.' They'll see it at 2am, stop what they're doing, and stare at the ceiling — because Ting's line is the thing they can't say out loud, but live every single day.
variations: Ting eventually started sleeping early. A month later, the doctor asked how she felt. She said: 'I sleep well. But I feel like I've disappeared.' The doctor nodded. 'Congratulations. That's the cost of being healthy.'

items.sourceNote: 靈感來自「密碼重設與自我認知」的英文黑色幽默梗，改寫為台灣銀行 App 場景，對白與情節完全原創

zh-tw.content:
阿哲想登入銀行 App，忘了密碼。

App 跳出提示：
「請回答您的安全問題：
您人生中最快樂的一天是哪一天？」

阿哲愣住了。

他想了三分鐘。

試著輸入「結婚那天」——錯誤。
試著輸入「兒子出生那天」——錯誤。
試著輸入「升上經理那天」——錯誤。

第四次嘗試他停下來，
看著螢幕。

突然發現，
他根本想不起來，
當初是哪一天設定的這個答案。

那天的他，
顯然知道一個正確答案。

而現在的他，
連那個版本的自己
都認不出來了。

他點了「忘記安全問題」，
決定改用指紋登入。

人類已經放棄回答自己是誰，
但指紋還記得。
zh-tw.editorNote: 傳給那個說「我覺得我跟以前不一樣了」的朋友——他會看完之後沉默很久，因為阿哲那個瞬間，是每個成年人都默默經歷過、但從沒有正式承認的告別
variations: 後來阿哲想：當年那個知道答案的自己，要是今天遇到現在的自己，大概也會覺得對方是冒名頂替的吧。

en.content:
Zac tried to log into his banking app and forgot the password.

The app popped up:
"Please answer your security question:
What was the happiest day of your life?"

Zac froze.

He thought for three minutes.

He typed "my wedding day" — wrong.
"the day my son was born" — wrong.
"the day I got promoted" — wrong.

On the fourth try, he stopped
and stared at the screen.

He suddenly realized
he couldn't remember
which day he originally picked.

That version of him
clearly knew the right answer.

This version of him
couldn't even recognize
who that guy was anymore.

He clicked "forgot security question"
and switched to fingerprint login.

Humans have given up
on answering who they are.
But the fingerprint still remembers.
en.editorNote: Send to the friend who keeps saying 'I don't feel like myself anymore.' They'll go quiet for a while — because Zac's moment is the silent goodbye every adult has been through, but never officially acknowledged.
